narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which evaluates applicants based on their likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated an 82.1% chance that you would not pass. This high predicted risk of failure triggered the rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting students with stronger odds of bar success to maintain high pass rates. For context, the model looks at factors like your undergraduate grades, early and later school performance deciles, LSAT score, family income, and whether you'd study full-time. In your case, as a Black male from a middle-income family planning to study full-time, the two biggest influences pushing toward this failure prediction were your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1st—well below the typical 5th or 6th decile—and your Year 1 decile also at 1st, again much lower than average. These low rankings in your school's grade distributions stood out as the strongest signals, suggesting challenges in coursework that often correlate with bar struggles for students in similar situations. Your LSAT score of 35, while solid, provided the main counterbalance by nudging the prediction slightly toward passing, as it's close to the average of about 37 but still showed some room for improvement compared to top performers. Overall, your undergraduate GPA of 2.90, below the typical 3.21, reinforced these concerns without being the top driver. Compared to students who typically get approved, who often rank in the middle or higher deciles early on and maintain momentum through later years, your profile highlighted early and persistent academic hurdles that the model sees as key bar exam risks, even with your full-time commitment. This doesn't reflect your full potential or personal story—many students from similar backgrounds overcome these metrics through targeted effort. To move forward, focus on retaking the LSAT to boost it above average, strengthening your recent academic decile by excelling in upper-level courses or a post-bac program, and gathering strong recommendation letters that speak to your resilience. Consider community college or targeted prep courses to rebuild your decile rankings, and reapply after demonstrating upward trends—schools value growth stories like yours. With persistence, you can shift these predictions in your favor.
